bins.name: Expedited interconnection for flexible loads
bins.desc: American Terawatt says traditional large-load interconnection takes five to eight years and asks FERC to direct ISOs and RTOs to create expedited interconnection processes and enabling tariffs for defined large flexible loads.
quotes: American Terawatt urges the Commission to focus swiftly and pragmatically on reforms that can be adopted without delay and that will encourage the timely expansion of digital and energy infrastructure.

Traditional interconnection of large loads averages five years, and often up to eight years throughout the country. Our ability to bring compute for AI online is severely constrained by the availability of power.

Data-center developers willing to build flexible AI factories or retrofit existing sites with flexible load capabilities should have access to a significantly accelerated grid interconnection process.
FERC should direct independent system operators (ISOs) to develop expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads, with transparent criteria.

FERC should direct regional transmission organizations and ISOs to propose enabling, nondiscriminatory tariffs for participation in expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads.

bins.name: Flexible loads as the core reform
bins.desc: The filer centers its proposal on flexible loads, arguing off-peak capacity could host 100 GW and that grid-responsive data centers can throttle GPU power to curtail roughly half their draw without stopping workloads. It urges FERC to define qualifying flexible loads and regulate them rather than leaving the matter to individual grid operators.
quotes: American Terawatt urges the Commission to focus swiftly and pragmatically on reforms that can be adopted without delay and that will encourage the timely expansion of digital and energy infrastructure.

Existing off-peak grid capacity could accommodate an additional 100 GW of flexible loads, more than enough to cover data-center growth and other demand through 2028 and beyond, without needing new power plants.
by throttling GPU power and slowing computations rather than switching devices off entirely, grid-responsive AI data centers (GRAIDs) can maintain uptime SLAs without ever stopping workloads.
Including facility overhead, a 100-megawatt (MW) GRAIDs facility could curtail approximately 50 MW on demand, all while keeping workloads continuously running.

Data-center developers willing to build flexible AI factories or retrofit existing sites with flexible load capabilities should have access to a significantly accelerated grid interconnection process.
The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) has so far explicitly chosen not to regulate flexible loads, leaving them to individual grid operators. It’s time for that to change.
FERC should direct independent system operators (ISOs) to develop expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads, with transparent criteria.

FERC should direct regional transmission organizations and ISOs to propose enabling, nondiscriminatory tariffs for participation in expedited interconnection processes for defined large flexible loads.

To ensure clarity for both developers and grid operators and prevent a national patchwork of regulations, FERC should define qualifying flexible loads for the purposes of transmission-level interconnection and wholesale market participation.

bins.name: Rate-base incentives and up-front premium
bins.desc: American Terawatt argues cost-of-service rate-base regulation, not capital, drives slow and oversized projects. It proposes a predetermined up-front premium for accelerated interconnection so existing ratepayers face no short-term rate increase.
quotes: The core issue is not capital. The real constraint is incentives. Utilities operate under cost-of-service regulation, where capital expenditures must be approved by regulators and then added to “rate base” to earn a regulated return.

This structure rewards utilities for undertaking larger, slower, and more capital-intensive projects, because those projects expand rate base, even when smaller or faster solutions would deliver usable grid capacity sooner.
By charging a predetermined up-front premium for this form of accelerated interconnection, utilities need not incur increased rates for consumers in the short term.

bins.name: Behind-the-meter colocation as a stopgap
bins.desc: The filer characterizes developer-owned behind-the-meter gas plants colocated with data centers as expensive and risky, framing them as an untenable workaround that grid-based flexible interconnection should replace.
quotes: To secure power more quickly, data-center developers are increasingly resorting to expensive, risky solutions such as building and operating their own behind-the-meter natural gas power plants colocated with large datacenter campuses.

bins.name: FERC should regulate flexible loads
bins.desc: The filer says FERC has so far declined to regulate flexible loads and should now define qualifying flexible loads for transmission-level interconnection and wholesale market participation to avoid a national patchwork.
quotes: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) has so far explicitly chosen not to regulate flexible loads, leaving them to individual grid operators. It’s time for that to change.

To ensure clarity for both developers and grid operators and prevent a national patchwork of regulations, FERC should define qualifying flexible loads for the purposes of transmission-level interconnection and wholesale market participation.

bins.name: Premium funds upgrades, not ratepayers
bins.desc: American Terawatt proposes that an up-front premium paid by developers for accelerated interconnection cover the associated costs, so existing consumers do not bear short-term rate increases.
quotes: By charging a predetermined up-front premium for this form of accelerated interconnection, utilities need not incur increased rates for consumers in the short term.

bins.name: GPU power throttling for curtailment
bins.desc: The filer proposes grid-responsive AI data centers that throttle GPU power on demand-response signals instead of shutting devices off, so a 100 MW facility could curtail about 50 MW while keeping workloads running and uptime SLAs intact.
quotes: by throttling GPU power and slowing computations rather than switching devices off entirely, grid-responsive AI data centers (GRAIDs) can maintain uptime SLAs without ever stopping workloads.
Including facility overhead, a 100-megawatt (MW) GRAIDs facility could curtail approximately 50 MW on demand, all while keeping workloads continuously running.
